behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces a route within 1.5x optimal length for inputs up to 20 stops
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithm test with no external dependencies or I/O. The real failure (heuristic producing a poor route) is caught by running the actual algorithm against known TSP benchmarks. A mock would always pass, hiding algorithmic flaws. The test needs: synthetic or benchmark instances with known optimal solutions, the real heuristic implementation, and a length comparison—all in-process and deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Use standard TSP benchmark instances (e.g., from TSPLIB or generate small problems with computed optima via dynamic programming). Run the heuristic on each, verify: (a) all nodes are visited exactly once, (b) total route length ≤ 1.5 × optimal length for that instance. Edge cases: 1-node, 2-node, symmetric vs. asymmetric distances.

behaviors.description: Delivery time windows are computed correctly from distance and road-type-specific average speed
behaviors.justification: The core behavior is a formula: time = distance ÷ speed. The real failure is a calculation error (e.g., multiplying instead of dividing, using wrong speed for road type). Testing the formula with known inputs/outputs is Unit level—no network, no database, no I/O. If the system integrates this with upstream geocoding or distance calculation, those have their own tests; this behavior isolates the estimation logic itself.
behaviors.test_description: Parameterized test with known distances, road types, and expected times. Example: distance=10km, road_type='highway', avg_speed=80km/h → time=7.5min. Test: (a) correct formula application, (b) road-type-to-speed mapping (config-driven), (c) unit conversion (km→m, hours→minutes), (d) edge cases (zero distance, very high/low speeds, unknown road types).

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects routes exceeding driver's remaining shift hours and reports excess time
behaviors.justification: This is a pure validation function: compare total_time against shift_hours, reject if over, compute and return excess. No I/O, no coordination with other modules. Real failures are logic errors: wrong comparison operator (> vs. ≥), off-by-one, incorrect excess calculation, missing rejection. All are caught by Unit-level tests with synthetic inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Test the validator function in isolation with known inputs: (a) total_time > shift_hours → rejects, excess is positive and correct, (b) total_time == shift_hours → edge case (passes or fails per spec), (c) total_time < shift_hours → accepts, no excess. Test: incorrect operator, negative/zero values, large values, boundary conditions.

behaviors.description: When one address fails geocoding (no results), service skips that stop, optimizes remaining addresses, and reports which addresses were skipped
behaviors.justification: This tests error-handling orchestration: multiple modules (input handler, geocoding client, failure handler, TSP, reporting) must cooperate. Mocking the geocoding API to return failure for one address is necessary (System test with real API would be impractical). Real failures: skipping the wrong address, not skipping, crashing instead of handling, incorrect reporting. These surface at the Integration boundary where modules interact, not in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the geocoding API to return no results for address N out of M. Call service with M addresses. Assert: (a) address N is removed from the route optimization, (b) TSP is run on M-1 addresses, (c) returned route and time estimates do not reference address N, (d) skipped addresses list includes address N by original input. Test: no addresses fail, all fail, first/last/middle address fails.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items
behaviors.justification: Real-world duplicate detection likely depends on geocoding (e.g., '123 Main St' and '123 Main Street' → same coordinates after geocoding), not just string matching. This requires integration with the geocoding module. Real failures: missing semantic duplicates (different strings, same location), consolidating wrong items, skipping duplicates entirely. A Unit test (string matching only) would miss these. A test with mocked geocoding returning identical coordinates for some addresses is Integration level.
behaviors.test_description: Provide address list with exact duplicates (e.g., '123 Main St' twice) and semantic duplicates (e.g., '123 Main St' and '123 Main Street'). Mock geocoding to return identical coordinates for duplicates. Assert: (a) exactly one stop per unique location (by geocoded coordinate), (b) delivery item counts/lists are merged correctly, (c) each item from all duplicate addresses is included in the consolidated stop. Test: no duplicates, all same address, mixed exact and semantic duplicates.
